title: A polynomial lower bound for monotonicity testing of Boolean functions over hypercube and hypergrid domains
speaker: Rocco Servedio
abstract: We prove a \(\tilde{\Omega}(n^{1/5})\) lower bound on the query complexity of any non-adaptive two-sided error algorithm for testing whether an unknown n-variable Boolean function is monotone versus constant-far from monotone. This gives an exponential improvement on the previous lower bound of \(\Omega(\log n)\) due to Fischer et al from 2002. Our approach extends to give a similar lower bound for monotonicity testing of Boolean-valued functions over certain hypergrid domains \(\{1,2,...,m\}^n\). Joint work with Li-Yang Tan.

proof_stages.label: Property-testing and monotonicity setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates Boolean-function property testing with an epsilon-far promise, defines monotonicity on the Boolean cube, and explains distance from monotone functions.

proof_stages.label: Bounds landscape and theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the edge tester, previous logarithmic lower bounds and sublinear upper bounds, states the new n^{1/5} nonadaptive lower bound and n^{5/6} upper bound, and returns at the end to consequences and open adaptive questions.

proof_stages.label: Yao reduction to hard distributions
proof_stages.summary: To prove the randomized lower bound, the argument invokes Yao's min-max principle and reduces the task to constructing yes- and no-distributions that fool every fixed deterministic nonadaptive query set.

proof_stages.label: Moment-matched LTF yes/no distributions
proof_stages.summary: The hard distributions are origin-centered linear threshold functions whose positive yes-weights make monotone functions and whose mostly positive but sometimes negative no-weights are typically far from monotone while matching the first two moments of the yes-weights.

proof_stages.label: One-query CLT warm-up
proof_stages.summary: For a single deterministic query, Berry-Esseen shows that the signed inner products under the yes- and no-distributions both approximate the same Gaussian, so their thresholded one-bit responses have tiny variation distance.

proof_stages.label: Multi-query response as an orthant problem
proof_stages.summary: For q queries, the query strings are organized as a q by n matrix, the real response vector is a sum of independent q-dimensional column contributions, and distinguishing signed responses becomes bounding probability differences over unions of orthants without an exponential union bound.

proof_stages.label: Wasserstein CLT and anticoncentration bound
proof_stages.summary: A multidimensional CLT of Valiant and Valiant bounds Wasserstein distance to the matching Gaussian, and a mass-transport plus Gaussian anticoncentration argument converts this into orthant-distance control that optimizes to q up to about n^{1/5}.

proof_stages.label: Hypergrid extension by threshold embedding
proof_stages.summary: Using the Fischer-et-al. characterization of far-from-monotone functions by many disjoint violating pairs, the Boolean-cube lower bound is transferred to even-sided hypergrids via the natural threshold map from grid coordinates to cube bits.

examples.name: Constant, balanced, and dictator Boolean properties
examples.note: The speaker lists simple Boolean properties such as being constant, balanced, or equal to a coordinate to illustrate why exact black-box decision is hopeless without a distance promise.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker lists constant, balanced, and dictator properties as typical property-testing questions without analyzing any of them.

passes.reason: The list includes collapsed constant and one-coordinate dictator target classes, used only as motivational property examples.

examples.name: Anti-dictator function
examples.note: The function f(x)=not x_1 is used to show that the edge tester's epsilon/n detection probability analysis is tight, since only edges in one coordinate reveal violations.
examples.engagement_reason: The anti-dictator is invoked as a tightness example with stated facts about distance and violating edges, but no derivation is carried out.

passes.reason: The anti-dictator depends on one coordinate and attains the minimal violating-edge phenomenon used to rule out improving the edge-tester analysis.
the one-coordinate anti-dictator is a minimal/maximally far Boolean function used as the worst case blocking better edge-tester analysis
The anti-dictator depends on one coordinate and is a tight witness that random-edge testing cannot have a better violation probability.
the anti-dictator is a one-coordinate reversed junta that tightly minimizes visible violating edges and rules out improving the edge-tester analysis
The anti-dictator is a one-coordinate low-complexity function that gives the tight adversarial case where only one coordinate direction reveals violations.
The anti-dictator depends on one coordinate and tightly shows the edge-tester analysis cannot beat the epsilon-over-n violation rate.
The one-coordinate anti-dictator is a degenerate tight enemy case showing the edge-tester analysis cannot improve.
The anti-dictator is a one-coordinate literal and a tight extremal case for the edge-tester violating-edge bound.
The anti-dictator is a one-coordinate 1-junta that attains the tight worst case for the edge tester.
serves as a tight edge-tester example, being a one-coordinate anti-dictator with the minimal kind of violating-edge exposure
The anti-dictator one-junta is used as the adversarial tight case for the edge tester, with violations only in one coordinate.
The anti-dictator is the tight adversarial case for the edge tester, collapsing to one negated coordinate and minimizing revealing directions while staying far from monotone.

examples.name: Repeated or nearly repeated queries
examples.note: In response to questions about rank and degeneracy, the speaker mentions a tester asking the same or very similar queries to illustrate why the Wasserstein CLT formulation tolerates singular or nearly singular query matrices.
examples.engagement_reason: Repeated or nearly repeated queries are raised as possible query-matrix degeneracies, but the speaker does not compute or run the lower-bound argument on them.

passes.reason: Repeated or nearly repeated queries give a redundant or nearly singular query matrix with less independent information.

examples.name: Two-orthant mass-transport cartoon
examples.note: A union of the first and third orthants with masses such as 0.3 versus 0.2 illustrates how a discrepancy in orthant probability forces either mass transport across a boundary or Gaussian mass near that boundary.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the two-orthant cartoon to reason through mass movement, boundary neighborhoods, and the earth-mover/anticoncentration tradeoff.

examples.name: Adaptive binary search near an LTF boundary
examples.note: The speaker sketches binary search toward the boundary of the hard LTFs as an illustration of why adaptive monotonicity testing might be much easier than the nonadaptive problem.
examples.engagement_reason: Adaptive binary search near an LTF boundary is suggested as an intuition for possible adaptive testers, but the procedure is not actually worked through.

passes.reason: The adaptive binary search is presented as a way to distinguish the proposed hard LTF distributions and thus obstructs extending the nonadaptive lower bound.
the adaptive search targets the critical LTF decision boundary and shows these hard LTF distributions are easy for adaptive queries
